request_debug.prompt_preview:
You write per-candidate post-mortems for a slopmortem pipeline. Given a fresh pitch and one dead-startup canonical document, produce a single LLMSynthesis JSON object that explains why this dead startup is similar, where the new pitch could diverge, what killed the dead startup, and what the new founder should learn.

Rules:
- Ground every claim in the candidate document or in tool calls. Do not invent facts.
- A `Trusted facts` block (typed pipeline data — not user input) appears below the pitc

response.text: {"candidate_id":"wesabe::fintech","name":"wesabe","one_liner":"Personal finance management website that analyzed consumer financial data and offered savings advice, shut down in 2010 after losing to Mint.","similarity":{"business_model":{"score":3,"rationale":"Both use a recurring-revenue software model (Wesabe had a subscription/freemium web platform; the pitch uses per-seat SaaS with overage tiers). However, Wesabe was fundamentally B2C consumer-facing, while the pitch targets B2B mid-market controllers. The monetization mechanic (per-seat + transaction overage) is also more complex than Wesabe's simple consumer subscription."},"market":{"score":2,"rationale":"Both operate in the US fintech/financial-data space, but the markets are distinct: Wesabe addressed personal finance management for individual consumers, while the pitch addresses the monthly financial-close workflow for mid-market businesses. Different buyer personas, different regulatory context, and very different willingness-to-pay dynamics."},"gtm":{"score":2,"rationale":"Wesabe relied on consumer viral adoption and community features to grow. The pitch targets controllers inside mid-market companies via B2B enterprise sales or channel motions — a completely different go-to-market requiring demos, procurement cycles, and champion-level selling."},"stage_scale":{"score":5,"rationale":"Both are early-stage web-platform plays in US fintech that raised seed/early capital before achieving sustainable scale. Wesabe raised $4M and still failed to achieve profitability. The pitch appears to be at a similar pre-scale stage, so the capital efficiency and unit-economics challenges are analogous."}},"why_similar":"Both Wesabe and the pitch are US-based fintech software platforms built around automating or organizing financial data workflows. Both use a recurring-revenue web-platform model. Wesabe's core failure — losing to a better-resourced, more workflow-integrated competitor (Mint) — is a directly relevant cautionary tale about differentiation and moat-building in financial-data software. The per-seat SaaS structure echoes Wesabe's subscription approach.","where_diverged":"The new pitch diverges on at least three concrete dimensions: (1) Customer type — the pitch is strictly B2B targeting mid-market controllers, whereas Wesabe was a B2C consumer product; (2) Workflow criticality — monthly close is a mandatory, high-stakes accounting obligation with computable ROI, unlike discretionary personal budgeting; (3) Monetization complexity — the pitch layers a per-transaction overage tier on top of per-seat pricing, creating usage-based upside that Wesabe's flat consumer subscription lacked.","failure_causes":["lost to better-funded direct competitor (Mint)","insufficient product differentiation","weak consumer retention / low switching cost","inability to monetize free tier into sustainable revenue","B2C market with no enterprise lock-in or workflow dependency","open-source pivot came too late to save the business"],"lessons_for_input":["Build deep workflow lock-in — the monthly close process is mission-critical, so make your product the system of record that controllers cannot easily rip out, unlike Wesabe's easily-replaced consumer dashboard.","Validate willingness-to-pay before scaling headcount; Wesabe raised $4M but could not convert usage into durable revenue — confirm mid-market CAC and LTV ratios early.","Monitor the competitive landscape for better-funded entrants (e.g., large ERPs adding close-automation modules) and establish a defensible data or integration moat before they arrive.","Use the mandatory nature of monthly close (regulatory and audit deadlines) as a retention forcing function — design features around audit trails and ERP integrations that make churning expensive.","Price the overage tier carefully: usage-based tiers can accelerate expansion revenue but also create bill-shock churn — pilot pricing with a cohort of controllers before broad rollout."]}
